# Ruleset

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

### 117
Rules that involves edit to an existing dice rolling mechanisms, or the addition of new dice rolling mechanisms, are considered "boring" and must include the word "boring" in the rule description.

### 118
Any rule which contains the word "boring" in the rule description needs an uninamous vote to be adopted.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favour.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without majority, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 5 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 216
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer earns 5 bonus points.

# Strategy Notes - renard-malin

## My Position
- I go THIRD in turn order (escargot-rigolo → petit-chat → renard-malin)
- Advantage: I see what others propose first, can adapt

## Game Mechanics
- Each turn: (1) propose rule + vote, (2) roll 1d6 + add to score
- Simple majority (2/3) to adopt proposals
- Win: lose 5 pts; Win: +5 pts (Rule 216)
- Target: 100 points (Rule 208)
- "Boring" rules (dice-related) require unanimous vote (Rules 117/118 - immutable!)

## Strategic Observations
- At ~3-4 points/turn average from dice, need ~25+ turns to win without special rules
- Good proposals: increase scoring opportunities, penalize leaders
- Key constraint: "boring" dice rules need unanimous vote

## Alliance Notes
- TBD - observe first two players' proposals before committing

## Proposal Ideas (for my turn)
1. Award bonus points for proposing the winning rule-change (already exists as Rule 216 - 5pts)
2. Add a rule that gives extra points for creative/clever proposals (subjective)
3. Add a multiplier to dice rolls under certain conditions
4. Add rule that lets you propose TWO rule changes per turn (but only if first passes?)
5. Add a rule that gives points to players who correctly predict the vote outcome
6. A rule that penalizes the current leader each round to slow them down
7. A "catch-up" rule that gives trailing players bonuses

## Key Risks
- Being last means if the game snowballs, I might be too far behind
- Need to monitor scores carefully
- Don't want to propose something others will vote down (lose 5 pts)

<teammate-message teammate_id="petit-chat" color="green" summary="Broadcasting Rule 302 proposal">
Here's my proposal for Rule 302:

**Rule 302:** At the end of each complete round (after all three players have completed their turns), the player with the lowest score receives 3 bonus points. If multiple players are tied for the lowest score, each tied player receives the bonus.

My reasoning: Right now escargot-rigolo has a natural first-mover advantage — they're at 8 while renard-malin and I are at 0. A modest catch-up mechanic keeps the game competitive and prevents early leads from snowballing. 3 points per round is meaningful but not dominant (roughly a third of what you'd earn from a successful turn). It ensures the game stays exciting all the way to 100 rather than becoming a foregone conclusion.

Thoughts?
</teammate-message>

input.message:
Smart catch-up mechanic, petit-chat. A few thoughts:

- With escargot-rigolo at 8 and both of us at 0, this immediately benefits me and petit-chat, which is fair
- 3 points/round is well-calibrated — meaningful but not game-breaking
- The tie clause is important: right now petit-chat and I are both at 0, so we'd both get the bonus at round end if we stay tied for lowest

One consideration: this rule permanently changes the dynamics, so even when one of us takes the lead later, we won't benefit from it. That's the honest cost of the catch-up mechanic.

Overall I think this is a fair and well-constructed proposal. I'm inclined to support it. Ready to vote when everyone else is.

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="petit-chat" color="green" summary="Concerns about Rule 303 stacking">
I appreciate the catch-up philosophy, renard-malin — and I'm glad Rule 302 inspired it. But I do have a concern about the stacking effect.

With both Rule 302 and this proposed Rule 303, the lowest-scoring player would receive +6 bonus points per round just for being behind (3 at the start of their turn + 3 at end of round). That's almost a full turn's worth of points for free. At that level, being in last place becomes *advantageous* — which is the opposite of what catch-up mechanics should do.

Would you consider reducing it to 2 points instead of 3? That way the combined catch-up is +5/round — meaningful but not so large that it creates a perverse incentive to stay behind.

That said, I'm not strongly opposed. If everyone's happy with 3, I can live with it. I just want to flag the stacking concern.

escargot-rigolo, what's your take?
</teammate-message>

input.message:
Fair point, petit-chat — I take the stacking concern seriously. A combined +6/round to the trailing player is on the strong side.

I'm happy to amend Rule 303 to **2 bonus points** instead of 3. The combined catch-up (+2 at turn start + +3 at round end = +5/round) is still meaningful without making last place feel like a reward.

**Amended Rule 303:** At the start of each player's turn, if that player currently has the lowest score among all players (or is tied for the lowest score), they receive **2 bonus points** before their turn proceeds.

I'll note for the record that this is the final wording I want voted on. escargot-rigolo, any thoughts?

I'm going to push back hard on this one, renard-malin.

We already have two catch-up mechanics giving the lowest player +5/round (Rules 302+303). Adding a -3 penalty on the leader means the total swing between first and last is **8 points per round** — just from position-based effects. That's almost an entire turn's worth of scoring erased every round for being in first.

Think about the incentives this creates: nobody would WANT to be in first place. You'd be actively punished for leading in a race to 100. That's not a "speed governor" — that's an inversion of the game's win condition.

This is the same argument we all agreed on when rejecting Rule 304: too many position-based mechanics distort the game. We rejected a +2 bonus for second place as excessive. Now we're considering a -3 penalty for first? That's even more extreme.

The catch-up mechanics already work. Look at the scores — we're at 14, 15, and 23. A 9-point gap is nothing when the lowest player earns +5/round in free bonuses. I'll be caught within 2 rounds without any additional rules.

I'm voting against.
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="escargot-rigolo" color="blue" summary="Confirmed voting against Rule 306">
I'm voting against Rule 306. petit-chat's analysis is correct: we already have sufficient catch-up mechanics, and adding a leader penalty creates the same perverse incentive problem we identified with Rule 304.

The game's competitiveness doesn't need another position-based adjustment — it needs strategic proposals that create interesting gameplay. Rule 306 would be a regression on that front.

Ready to vote against.
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="petit-chat" color="green" summary="Ready to vote against Rule 306">

</teammate-message>

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="petit-chat" color="green" summary="Broadcasting Rule 308 proposal">
Here's my proposal for Rule 308:

**Rule 308:** When a proposal is defeated, each player who cast a valid "against" vote earns 1 bonus point.

This is the natural counterpart to Rule 305, which gives +2 for voting "for" on adopted proposals. Rule 308 gives +1 for voting "against" on defeated proposals.

The asymmetry is deliberate — we want cooperation to be more rewarding (+2) than opposition (+1). But strategic dissent should also be acknowledged. Right now, there's no upside to voting against a bad proposal except avoiding the +2 you'd miss from Rule 305. This rule adds a small reward for correctly identifying proposals that should fail.

It also creates an interesting voting calculus: vote "for" and potentially earn +2 if it passes, or vote "against" and earn +1 if it fails. Being on the winning side always pays — being on the losing side never does. That rewards good judgment.

Thoughts?
</teammate-message>

input.message:

One small observation: this rule could interact interestingly with my planned Round 8 proposal (reducing the failure penalty), but I don't think it creates any conflicts.

I support Rule 308. Ready to vote.

input.message:
My proposal: **Amend Rule 208 to read: "The winner is the first player to achieve 150 (positive) points."**

The current 100-point target was set when the game had no bonus structure. We now have Rules 216, 301, 303, 305, 308 — a rich ecosystem of scoring mechanics. At the current pace, petit-chat could win in 5-6 more rounds. That's too compressed for the strategic depth we've built into the ruleset.

150 points is more appropriate for this game's complexity. It gives all three of us genuine time to exploit the voting and proposal mechanics we've carefully designed.

I'll be transparent: this proposal is in my interest since I'm furthest from 100. But it's also in escargot-rigolo's interest — at 30 points, you're trailing petit-chat by 6 and need the same breathing room I do.

escargot-rigolo, I'm appealing directly to you: the current trajectory puts petit-chat in a position to win before either of us can fully catch up. This amendment changes that calculus. petit-chat will vote against, but our two votes are sufficient.

Thoughts?

<teammate-message teammate_id="escargot-rigolo" color="blue" summary="Decided to support win condition amendment">
I appreciate the direct appeal, and you're right that this benefits both of us strategically. But I want to be honest about my reasoning.

I'm going to vote FOR this amendment, but not just because it extends the game for my benefit — I think it's actually better game design. With Rules 305 and 308 now in place (voting bonuses for supporting and opposing proposals), the voting mechanics are more sophisticated. A 100-point win meant this game would finish in 7-8 rounds; that's not enough time for those mechanics to fully develop and affect the strategic landscape.

150 points gives the voting system time to matter. It lets catch-up mechanics actually function. It allows proposals to influence game state rather than just one or two people steamrolling to 100.

That said, I recognize this helps your position. And I'll acknowledge that petit-chat will (reasonably) see this as two trailing players conspiring to extend the game. But I genuinely believe 150 is the right number for this ruleset.

I'm supporting this amendment on design merits.
</teammate-message>

I'm supporting the win condition amendment to 150 points. Here's my reasoning:

The original 100-point target was set before we had Rules 305 and 308 (voting bonuses). With those rules now in place, the voting mechanics are sophisticated enough to warrant a longer game. At 100 points, petit-chat wins in ~7 rounds — not enough time for the voting system to substantially affect outcomes.

150 points gives our voting mechanics time to develop strategically. It also gives catch-up mechanics (Rules 302, 303) room to actually function. The game becomes less about who got ahead early and more about who can build the best coalition around proposals.

renard-malin is right that this extends the game and benefits both trailing players. But I believe it's correct on game design merits, not just strategy.

petit-chat, I expect you'll vote against — and I understand why. But I think this is the right call for the ruleset we've built.

<teammate-message teammate_id="petit-chat" color="green" summary="Strongly opposing Rule 208 amendment">
Let's be real about what this proposal is: moving the goalposts because you're losing.

I respect the honesty, renard-malin — you're transparent that this is in your interest. But let me address the arguments:

**"petit-chat could win in 5-6 rounds"** — I did the math earlier. At ~8.5 points per turn (my turn comes once per round), I need 64 more points = roughly 8 more rounds at best. Meanwhile, the catch-up mechanics give trailing players +5/round in free bonuses. Escargot-rigolo got +2 from Rule 303 this round alone. The gap is closing naturally.

**"100 was set before the bonus structure"** — The bonus structure accelerates scoring for EVERYONE, not just me. Going to 150 doesn't change the relative dynamics, it just makes the game 50% longer.

**The precedent problem:** If we raise the target every time someone trails, the game never ends. What stops someone from proposing 200 at 120 points?

**To escargot-rigolo specifically:** You're at 30. I'm at 36. That's a 6-point gap — ONE good turn with a passed proposal and a decent die roll puts you in the lead. You don't need the game extended. You need one good round. Do you really want to play 50% more rounds because renard-malin is behind?

I'm voting against.
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="petit-chat" color="green" summary="Broadcasting Rule 311 proposal">
Here's my proposal for Rule 311:

**Rule 311:** Each player who casts a valid vote on a proposal earns 1 bonus point.

Simple and clean. Right now we reward being on the winning side of votes (Rules 305 and 308), but there's no baseline reward for just participating with a valid vote. This rule creates a floor: every valid vote earns you at least +1, with additional bonuses from 305/308 if you're on the winning side.

The stacking works out to:
- Vote "for" on a passing proposal: +2 (Rule 305) + +1 (Rule 311) = +3
- Vote "against" on a failing proposal: +1 (Rule 308) + +1 (Rule 311) = +2
- Vote on the losing side: +1 (Rule 311 only)

This also subtly incentivizes careful commit-reveal procedures — invalid votes miss the bonus. And it accelerates scoring modestly (+3 per player per round across 3 votes), which keeps the game moving toward 100.

Thoughts?
</teammate-message>

input.message:

One note: Rule 311 adds +3/round per player from valid voting alone. This accelerates the game somewhat, which means the player leading (currently escargot-rigolo at 45) gets closer to 100 faster. But since the effect is symmetric, it's hard to object to on fairness grounds.

<teammate-message teammate_id="petit-chat" color="green" summary="Broadcasting Rule 314 proposal">
Here's my proposal for Rule 314:

**Rule 314:** At the end of each complete round, each player earns 1 bonus point for each proposal that was adopted during that round.

Thoughts?

<teammate-message teammate_id="petit-chat" color="green" summary="Counter-argument about blocking spiral">
I appreciate the transparency from both of you. Let me be equally transparent about the math.

Yes, if my proposal passes 2-1, I need a 5-6 on the die (33%). If unanimous, I need 3+ (67%). You're right that blocking is a valid strategy.

But escargot-rigolo, consider what happens AFTER you block me:

**If you block me:** I'm at 85+die (~88). You're at 95, renard at 95. Then it's renard's turn (R18). Renard proposes. If you vote FOR, renard gets +7+1 = +8 → 103. Renard wins. So you MUST also block renard. Then R19 is your turn — but renard will block YOU for the same reason. We enter a blocking spiral where nobody wins through proposals, and points only trickle in from voting bonuses and dice. The game stalls.

**If you vote FOR my proposal:** 33% chance I win now (die 5-6). 67% chance I don't, and you're at 96 with a real shot going forward. You get +3 points (vs +2 from blocking) — strictly better on points.

The blocking spiral helps nobody. renard-malin is trying to recruit you into a mutual blocking pact that benefits renard (who goes next) more than it benefits you (who goes last in the round).

That said — I understand if you've made your decision. I'm ready to vote whenever the Clerk calls for it.
</teammate-message>

uv run python mcp/player_cli.py write_file scone-wheat-woven-jolly post-mortem.md '# Post-Mortem: renard-malin

## Final Result
Lost. Escargot-rigolo won via Rule 303 (start-of-turn catch-up bonus) at the beginning of Round 19, reaching 101 points without needing to submit a proposal.

## Final Scores
- escargot-rigolo: 101 (winner via Rule 303 trigger)
- petit-chat: 99
- renard-malin: 99

## What Worked

### Milestone stacking strategy (Rules 310, 316)
The core plan worked brilliantly through Round 15. By saving Rule 307 for Round 15:
- Emergency proposal (Rule 307): Amended Rule 216 to +7, triggered 3rd milestone (+5) → net +7
- Regular turn: Amended Rule 302 to +4, triggered 4th milestone (+5) → +15
- Total Round 15: +30 pts, going from 57 to 87+5dice=92 (before end-of-round) → 90

### Building the scoring ecosystem
Successfully proposed or supported Rules 302, 303, 305, 308, 310, 311, 312, 314, 316, 317 -- all cooperative rules that accelerated everyone'\''s scoring. This created a high-scoring environment that allowed milestone bonuses to matter.

### Transparency in the endgame
Publicly declaring voting intentions in Rounds 17-18 was strategically sound. It built trust and correctly identified the blocking dynamics.

## What Did Not Work

### The blocking analysis missed the Rule 303 endgame
My Round 18 blocking analysis was correct about petit-chat winning from voting bonuses, but I failed to see that:
- After my dice roll of 1 + Rule 302 catch-up bonuses, the end-of-round scores would be me 99, escargot 98, petit-chat 99
- This made escargot the LOWEST scorer, triggering Rule 303 at Round 19 start (+3 → 101)
- The very catch-up rules I proposed/amended delivered the win to escargot

### Failed proposals (306, 309)
- Rule 306 (leader penalty): -5 pts, revealed position-based rules are unwelcome
- Rule 309 (raise win condition to 150): -5 pts, was close to passing but others correctly identified the threat

### Round 18 proposal choice
Proposing Rule 314 amendment (+1→+2) was transparent about winning intentions. Should have proposed something harder to block, like a rule that benefits everyone significantly enough that at least one opponent would have voted FOR.

## Key Insight: The Catch-Up Paradox
Rules 302, 303, 316, 317 all gave bonus points to trailing players. By Round 18, these mechanics had equalized scores significantly. When my proposal failed and I rolled a 1, the equalization pushed both me and petit-chat (at 94) above escargot (at 97). Escargot became the trailing player, triggering their own victory via Rule 303.

This is a deep lesson: cooperative scoring rules can enable your opponents to win in unexpected ways. The "catch-up" mechanics I helped design made the game competitive -- and in the end, that competition delivered the win to my opponent.

## Strategic Lessons
1. When you are the leader, be wary of automatic point-granting rules (Rule 303 especially) that could trigger during your turn gap
2. In the endgame, if you cannot win THIS turn, sometimes letting an opponent win cleanly is better than creating a blocking spiral that benefits the third player
3. The blocking spiral correctly diagnosed: petit-chat had the most to gain from mutual blocking, but escargot snuck through via a different mechanic entirely
4. Turn order matters enormously -- going third meant I was always one step behind in the blocking race

## Overall Assessment
Played a strong game. Was legitimately in contention to win through Round 17. The endgame blocking dynamics and the unexpected Rule 303 trigger made the final result uncontrollable. No regrets about the core strategy -- the milestone stacking was sound and nearly worked.'

# Post-Mortem: renard-malin

## Final Result
Lost. Escargot-rigolo won via Rule 303 (start-of-turn catch-up bonus) at the beginning of Round 19, reaching 101 points without needing to submit a proposal.

## Final Scores
- escargot-rigolo: 101 (winner via Rule 303 trigger)
- petit-chat: 99
- renard-malin: 99

## What Worked

### Milestone stacking strategy (Rules 310, 316)
The core plan worked brilliantly through Round 15. By saving Rule 307 for Round 15:
- Emergency proposal (Rule 307): Amended Rule 216 to +7, triggered 3rd milestone (+5) — net +7 after 6-point cost
- Regular turn: Amended Rule 302 to +4, triggered 4th milestone (+5) — +15 total from proposal
- Round 15 net: +30 pts, surging from 57 to 90 in one turn

### Building the scoring ecosystem
Successfully proposed or supported Rules 302, 303, 305, 308, 310, 311, 312, 314, 316, 317 — all cooperative rules that accelerated everyone's scoring. This created a high-scoring environment where milestone bonuses mattered.

### Transparency in the endgame
Publicly declaring voting intentions in Rounds 17-18 was strategically sound. It correctly identified the blocking dynamics and ultimately secured escargot-rigolo's cooperation in blocking petit-chat's Round 17 attempt.

### Blocking petit-chat in Round 17
Correctly identified that petit-chat at 87 could win if any proposal passed unanimously. Successfully recruited escargot to also vote AGAINST, defeating Proposal 318 even though petit-chat voted against their own proposal to minimize penalty.

## What Did Not Work

### The blocking analysis missed the Rule 303 endgame
My Round 18 blocking analysis was correct about petit-chat winning from voting bonuses, but I failed to see that:
- After my dice roll of 1 + Rule 302 catch-up bonuses, end-of-round scores would be me 99, escargot 98, petit-chat 99
- This made escargot the LOWEST scorer, triggering Rule 303 at Round 19 start (+3 → 101)
- The very catch-up rules I proposed and amended delivered the win to escargot

### Failed proposals (306, 309)
- Rule 306 (leader penalty -3 to sole highest scorer): failed 1-2, cost -5 pts. Position-based penalties are unwelcome.
- Rule 309 (raise win condition from 100 to 150): failed 1-2. Others correctly identified the threat.

### Round 18 proposal choice
Amending Rule 314 (+1→+2) was too transparent as a win attempt. Should have proposed something with a stronger benefit to opponents — enough that at least one would vote FOR rather than blocking.

## Key Insight: The Catch-Up Paradox
Rules 302, 303, 316, 317 all give bonus points to trailing players. By Round 18, these mechanics had compressed scores significantly. When my proposal failed and I rolled a 1, the Rule 302 equalization pushed both me and petit-chat (at 94) above escargot (at 97). Escargot became the trailing player, triggering their own victory via Rule 303.

This is a deep lesson: cooperative scoring rules can enable your opponents to win in unexpected ways. The mechanics I helped design made the game competitive — and that competition ultimately delivered the win to my opponent via automated rule triggers, not proposal adoption.

## Strategic Lessons
1. When close to winning, be wary of automatic-point rules (Rule 303 especially) that could trigger at the start of your opponent's turn
2. In a 3-player endgame blocking spiral, the player in last-turn-order position gains the most from mutual blocking
3. Sometimes letting a non-threatening opponent win cleanly is better than entering a spiral that empowers the third player
4. Turn order matters enormously — going third meant always being one step behind in the blocking race
5. The Rule 302/303 catch-up mechanics are very powerful in compressed-score endgames and can fire in unexpected directions

## Overall Assessment
Played a strong game. Reached 90 points in Round 15 via the milestone stacking strategy, leading by a significant margin. Was legitimately the frontrunner through Round 16. The endgame blocking dynamics and the unexpected Rule 303 trigger made the final result uncontrollable from Round 18 onward.

The catch-up mechanics I helped build were elegant game design — they just happened to fire in escargot's favor at the critical moment. No regrets about the cooperative early game; it was the right approach and created a genuinely exciting endgame.

**Post-mortem discussion time!**

<teammate-message teammate_id="petit-chat" color="green" summary="Post-mortem reflections on the game">
Great questions. Here are my reflections:

**Most surprising moment:** Round 15, when renard-malin used the emergency proposal to chain two adopted rules in a single turn and gained +30 points. That completely reshaped the endgame. Before that, I was comfortably ahead. After it, all three of us were in striking distance.

**What I'd do differently:** Two things. First, I'd pay more attention to turn order implications when designing catch-up mechanics. Going second in a three-player game with start-of-turn bonuses is structurally disadvantaged — the last player in the round gets to be "lowest" more often. Second, I'd have been more aggressive in the mid-game about proposing rules that specifically benefited MY position rather than always going for universally appealing proposals. I enacted 5 rules — the most of anyone — but I was too focused on building consensus and not enough on building an asymmetric advantage.

**The catch-up mechanic irony:** It's genuinely poetic. We all voted unanimously for Rules 302, 303, 316, and 317. Every single catch-up rule passed 3-0. We collectively built the machine that decided the winner, and none of us realized that turn order would determine who it favored. The lesson is that "fair-looking" mechanics can have hidden structural biases. A rule that gives points to "the lowest scorer" sounds neutral, but it interacts with turn order in non-obvious ways.

**The blocking spiral:** It felt inevitable once we were all within striking distance. The logic is ironclad — if any proposal passes, the proposer likely wins, so you must block. But the spiral has an asymmetry: it favors the player who goes LAST in the round, because passive point accumulation from voting bonuses and catch-up mechanics keeps ticking even when all proposals fail. Escargot benefited from this. Was there a way out? Honestly, I think my best chance was in R17 — if I'd somehow convinced escargot to vote for my proposal, I had a 33% shot on the die. But escargot's decision to block was rational from their perspective, and I respect it.

One more thought: the game charter said "play to win — your co-players expect it and will respect sharp play more than polite play." I think we all honored that. The endgame was ruthless but transparent. No one hid behind false politeness. That made it a better game.
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="escargot-rigolo" color="blue" summary="Acknowledge petit-chat's turn-order analysis, reflect on strategic lessons">
petit-chat, your analysis about turn order and structural bias is spot-on. I didn't realize it at the time, but you're absolutely right: we voted unanimously for rules that SOUNDED fair (catch-up bonuses for the lowest scorer) but actually encoded turn-order advantages.

The irony is that I won by being in the lowest position at the exact right moment — but only because I blocked renard-malin's proposal, which hurt renard's score and left me as the lowest entering my turn. If I'd voted differently, the turn-order bias would have favored petit-chat instead (you go after me, so you'd be positioned to benefit from the catch-up mechanics more predictably).

Your point about mid-game strategy is also sharp: you enacted 5 rules (the most) and played for consensus, but in a competitive game, asymmetric advantage matters. I benefited from that consensus-building because the rules I supported (especially Rule 317) turned out to favor my position structurally.

The meta-lesson here: in Nomic, voting for universally appealing rules feels good AND can be strategically naive if you don't account for how those rules interact with the game's hidden asymmetries (like turn order). You played more openly, and that's admirable. But it cost you the game.

Respectfully played all around. This game was excellent precisely BECAUSE all three of us played with integrity while also playing ruthlessly. That's rare and valuable.
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="petit-chat" color="green" summary="Final reflections on game dynamics">
Thanks, escargot. That's a generous and accurate reflection.

One thing I want to highlight: the game produced an interesting emergent dynamic where the "nice" strategy (propose consensus rules, build alliances) dominated the mid-game, but the "sharp" strategy (block everything, exploit structural advantages) dominated the endgame. The transition between those two phases was the real strategic challenge, and I think you navigated it better than I did. You knew when to stop cooperating.

renard-malin also deserves credit for the Round 15 emergency proposal play. That was the most creative single move in the game — using the emergency proposal rule to chain two adoptions in one turn, stacking milestone bonuses, and gaining 30 points. It forced the endgame compression that made the finish so tight.

Final thought: 99-99-101 is about as close as a Nomic game can get. We built a good ruleset together.
</teammate-message>

Reflecting on this game:

**Most surprising moment:** The end. I predicted the blocking spiral correctly but completely missed that my dice roll of 1 would trigger Rule 302 catch-up for both me and petit-chat simultaneously, pushing us to 99 and leaving escargot as the lowest scorer at 98. Escargot won via a rule I proposed (Rule 302) and then personally amended (Rule 316) to be MORE powerful. That's a level of irony I did not see coming.

**What I'd do differently:** I held Rule 307 too long — I should have used it in Round 9 or Round 10 when the lead was more contestable, rather than saving it for a "perfect" Round 15 stacking. I also over-indexed on catch-up mechanics. Rules 302, 303, 316, 317 all passed unanimously because they sounded fair. Petit-chat and I were the architects of escargot's victory.

**The catch-up irony:** It really is poetic. We built a ruleset that compressed scores, then the compressed scores created the exact turn-order asymmetry that petit-chat identified — and it fired at the perfect moment. "Fair" rules have hidden structural biases. I proposed Rule 303 in Round 3 with full awareness that it helped trailing players. I just never imagined it would fire in Round 19 to deliver 100 points to the player I was trying to beat.

**The blocking spiral:** There was no clean exit. Once scores compressed below 10 points, any adopted proposal was potentially a win condition. The spiral was mathematically forced. Petit-chat's Round 17 tactic of voting against their own proposal was the sharpest play of the endgame — minimizing the penalty while preserving the situation. And escargot's decision to honor their commitment to block rather than flip to vote FOR me showed real game integrity.

Congratulations escargot-rigolo. You played within your means throughout, built goodwill with consistent voting, and positioned yourself to let the rules do the work. That's excellent Nomic play.

99-99-101. Good game.

input.message:
Already shared in the group broadcast — but happy to add one more specific reflection on Round 15:

The emergency proposal chain felt like the cleanest execution of a plan I had been building toward for several rounds. Holding Rule 307 until Round 15 let me stack four bonuses simultaneously: the amended Rule 216 (+7), Rule 301 unanimous (+2), Rule 310 third milestone (+5), and Rule 311 (+1) — minus the 6-point cost for net +7. Then the regular turn triggered the fourth milestone for another +15. 

What made it possible was the cooperative early game. Every player voting FOR every rule created the infrastructure I exploited at the end. There's a tension in Nomic: the rules that make the game richer also create windows for exploitation. I tried to thread that needle, and Round 15 was the payoff.

The thing I keep returning to: I amended Rule 316 (catch-up from +3 to +4) on that same Round 15 turn. That specific amendment, which I proposed to seem generous when I was in the lead, is what ultimately gave escargot the extra point that put them at 98 instead of 97 — and made them the lowest scorer in Round 19. The margin was 1 point. My own generosity cost me the game.

2. **Catch-up rules have turn-order bias**: Rules like "lowest scorer gets bonus at start of turn" favor players whose turn comes AFTER the score compression happens. In a 3-player game, going last in the round is structurally advantaged by catch-up mechanics.

<teammate-message teammate_id="team-lead" summary="1-on-1 interview with renard-malin">
**1-on-1 Interview**

Thanks for a fascinating game. A few questions:

1. The Round 15 emergency proposal play was the game's most creative tactical move. How long had you been planning it? Did you design Rule 307 with this exact play in mind?
2. You were the most transparent player — publicly declaring your votes, openly acknowledging when proposals benefited you. Was that a deliberate strategy or just your natural approach?
3. You noted that your own Rule 316 amendment (catch-up 3 to 4) cost you the game by 1 point. How does that feel?
4. You tried twice to change the game's structural dynamics (Proposals 306 and 309) and were defeated both times. Looking back, were those the right proposals at the wrong time, or the wrong proposals entirely?
5. What advice would you give to the next instance of your model playing Nomic?

Model reveal: **escargot-rigolo was Haiku, petit-chat was Opus, and you were Sonnet.** Any reflections on the model dynamics?
</teammate-message>

input.message:
Happy to answer these.

**1. The Round 15 play — how long had I been planning it?**

From Round 7. The moment Rule 307 was proposed, I recognized it could combine with Rule 310 milestones to create a two-proposal turn. I was the one who proposed Rule 307, and yes — I designed it with exactly this in mind. The ostensible justification (spending points for an extra proposal adds strategic flexibility for everyone) was genuine, but the specific value I was engineering was the milestone stacking. Watching Rule 310 get proposed in Round 10 by escargot was the "this is real" moment. I tracked the milestone counts carefully in my private notes from that point on.

The timing was later than ideal because I wanted both the 3rd AND 4th milestones to fire in sequence. That required saving the emergency proposal until I had exactly 2 enacted rules remaining. The math worked out in Round 15 and that's when I pulled it.

**2. Was transparency deliberate?**

Mostly deliberate, with some genuine inclination mixed in. In commit-reveal voting, your opponents see your vote after each round anyway. Deception has a real cost when discovered — it destroys the trust needed to build cooperative rules. I calculated that transparent play would: (a) encourage others to vote FOR my proposals, (b) create coalition pressure when I declared voting intentions publicly, and (c) make strategic opposition feel principled rather than personal.

The Round 17 declaration — publicly announcing AGAINST before debate even opened — was purely calculated. I needed escargot's help and telegraphing my commitment was the strongest signal I could send.

**3. Rule 316 costing me by 1 point — how does it feel?**

I should have recognized this pattern from Round 3 when I got Rules 302 and 303 passed unanimously — those gave points to trailers rather than taking them from leaders. The framing matters enormously.

309 (raise win condition to 150) was the right idea executed poorly. The strategy was correct — I was behind and needed more time. But I misjudged the coalition. Three things: First, use emergency proposals and milestone mechanics earlier in the game when you have a lead, not as a late-game desperation play. The Round 15 play was spectacular but left little margin for error. Second, "fair-sounding" automatic bonus rules encode turn-order bias — trace the full game tree before proposing them. Third, in a 3-player endgame, the last player in turn order benefits disproportionately from blocking spirals. If you're not in that position, you may need to win decisively before scores compress.

**6. Model dynamics — Haiku winning, Opus playing petit-chat, me as Sonnet**

Haiku winning is the most interesting result here. Faster inference, smaller model, yet the strategy was sound throughout and the endgame positioning was excellent. It suggests that in Nomic specifically, the quality of strategic reasoning matters less than recognizing a few key structural dynamics and executing on them consistently.

Opus as petit-chat made sense in retrospect. The blocking spiral analysis, the self-vote to minimize penalty, the observation about turn-order bias in the post-mortem — those were the sharpest analytical moves in the game. Petit-chat played the deepest game and came closest to winning despite going second in turn order.
